title: DecoderFinalize leaks pinned_input napi_ref when decoder is GC'd without dispose()
description: DecoderFinalize (called by the GC finalizer) iterates data->events and deletes them but does NOT call release_pinned_decoder. If a caller creates a decoder, pushes a chunk (pinning it via napi_create_reference), then lets the object be GC'd without calling dispose() or close(), the napi_ref in pinned_input is never deleted. This pins the JS ArrayBuffer indefinitely, preventing its memory from being freed by V8.

title: EncoderFinalize leaks multi-push encoder pixel/icc/exif/xmp/extra_channels/frames/custom_boxes vectors when GC'd without dispose()
description: EncoderFinalize (the GC finalizer) only deletes data->chunks refs and the pinned_input ref, then calls delete data. It does not shrink-to-empty the large pixel/metadata/frame vectors the way EncoderDispose does (via swap idiom). This means encode jobs that are abandoned mid-flight (no dispose() call) hold their full pixel memory until the allocator decides to reclaim it — which for large images can be tens of MB held in the C++ heap after V8 frees the JS side. In a long-running process this accumulates.

title: cachedBinding module-level singleton is written without guard after concurrent callers read it as null
description: loadNativeBinding() reads cachedBinding (line 268), then may perform expensive require() work across loop iterations, then writes cachedBinding (line 290). In Node.js this is safe for synchronous paths only. However, the function is exposed as part of a synchronous public API and tests show it is called from multiple createDecoder/createEncoder call sites that could be invoked concurrently (e.g. from worker_threads). If two JS threads call loadNativeBinding() simultaneously both see null, both load, and the second write stomps the first. The real risk is that the wrapped probe closure (lines 284-287) captures a different `candidate` than the one already cached, giving the wrong probe.path to later callers.

title: decoder.close() promise not awaited before expect() in decompression-bomb test — assertion may fire before rejection
description: The test at line 496 calls `expect(decoder.close()).rejects.toThrow(...)` but does not `await` the expectation. In Bun's test runner an unawaited `expect(...).rejects` assertion may resolve after the test function returns, silently passing even if the rejection never fires. The correct form is `await expect(decoder.close()).rejects.toThrow(...)`. The bug means the ImageTooLarge guard could be broken without the test catching it.

title: DecoderCancel sets flag but does not release pinned_input reference, leaving it pinned until close/dispose
description: DecoderCancel (line 1711) sets data->cancelled = true but does not call release_pinned_decoder. DecoderClose does call it on the cancel path (line 1646). However if a consumer calls cancel() and then never calls close() or dispose() — which is a valid usage pattern — the pinned napi_ref on the input ArrayBuffer leaks for the object's lifetime. The symmetric path for the encoder (EncoderCancel) has the same gap.

title: EncoderData memory leaked when custom box type validation fails mid-loop after earlier boxes already reserved capacity
description: In CreateEncoder (line 2163-2180), when a custom box has a type string of incorrect length the function calls `delete data` and returns an error. However at that point `data->extra_channels`, `data->frames`, `data->icc`, `data->exif`, `data->xmp` may already hold heap-allocated vectors, and the pinned_input ref (impossible here since pushPixels is not yet called) is null. The explicit `delete data` at line 2171 does invoke vector destructors properly, so this is not a leak — HOWEVER `release_pinned(env, data)` is called before delete when pinned_input could theoretically be non-null from a future code path, creating a fragile assumption. Current code is safe but the pattern is a maintenance trap.

title: wrapDecoder's push method is synchronous but NativeDecoder interface declares it as void | Promise<void> — callers that await push() and check for errors will silently swallow throws
description: The wrapped decoder's push method (line 382) is `(chunk) => raw.push(chunk)` — a plain forwarding function. If raw.push() throws synchronously the error propagates correctly. But the NativeDecoder interface type declares push as `void | Promise<void>`. If a caller does `await decoder.push(chunk)` and the native push throws, the throw surfaces synchronously before the await, which is correct. However callers that do `decoder.push(chunk).catch(...)` expecting a promise will get a TypeError because the return is void, not a Promise. This is a type/contract mismatch that can cause silent failures in async error paths.

title: NativeDecoder/NativeEncoder have no AbortSignal integration — cancel() must be called manually with no composable cancellation
description: Both NativeDecoder and NativeEncoder expose a cancel(reason?) method but there is no way to wire a standard AbortSignal (e.g. from AbortController or fetch timeouts) to trigger cancel automatically. In practice callers must manually bridge AbortSignal.addEventListener('abort', ...) to decoder.cancel(). Adding an optional signal parameter to createDecoder/createEncoder (or to the wrapDecoder shim) would allow safe composition with browser/Node cancellation patterns and prevent leaks when the surrounding request is aborted.
